title: The impact of social prescribing on well-being outcomes in a nationwide analysis
methods: conditional growth models with interaction terms
data_sources: PSSRU Costs of Health and Social Care
HM Treasury Green Book WELLBY metric

data_scope.platform: Access Elemental (cloud-based UK social prescribing platform)
data_scope.full_cohort_cases: over 600,000 SP cases from 482,575 patients (as of 25 April 2025)
data_scope.geography_northern_ireland: 80% of health and social care trusts
data_scope.geography_scotland: 13% of health and social care partnerships
data_scope.data_access: Third-party restricted; minimal dataset available from corresponding author on request

core_conclusion: A UK nationwide analysis of 14,657-19,627 patients across 300+ social prescribing sites demonstrates consistent, sizeable well-being improvements within 1-6 months of referral: SWEMWBS +3.31 points, happiness +1.59, life satisfaction +1.57, worthwhileness +1.40, anxiety -1.45. The life-satisfaction gain equates to a return on investment of £9 per £1 invested (2019 prices). Well-being improvements are broadly consistent across sociodemographic groups, supporting the universal applicability of social prescribing.
why_it_matters: First national-scale quantitative evidence of SP well-being impact spanning all UK referral routes and geographies; provides commissioning-ready economic evidence (£9 RoI) aligned with the HM Treasury Green Book; demonstrates broad equity of SP benefits across deprivation, age, sex, and urbanicity; showcases routine digital administrative platforms as viable national research infrastructure.
why_nature: Practice-changing policy evidence at national scale directly relevant to NHS neighbourhood health transformation; methodological advance over prior SP work (Bayesian growth curves with full uncertainty propagation, multiple sensitivity analyses); equity dimension elevates from service evaluation to public health significance; timely contribution at a pivotal moment for UK and global SP expansion.
added_value_to_my_research: Spatial stratification of SP outcomes via postcode-to-IMD linkage feeds directly into GWR/MGWR models of place-based health intervention effectiveness
Mobile-phone mobility traces as passive well-being proxies — calibrated against ONS4 ground truth — extend my spatiotemporal KDE and mobility-flow methods into health outcome estimation
Deprivation-weighted WELLBY-RoI surface operationalizes equity-weighted cost-effectiveness for spatial healthcare investment optimization, paralleling my EV-charging efficiency-equity framework
Fusing SP activity types with remote-sensing green-space indices (NDVI, park access 2SFCA) creates an explainable ML model of SP outcome determinants across environmental predictors

Causal ML (double/debiased ML, causal forests) applied to the 600k-patient Access Elemental cohort yields heterogeneous treatment-effect estimates across SP pathways — a transferable pipeline for evaluating spatial health interventions in my datasets

figures.caption: Predicted average pre-post trajectories and 95% HDIs for SWEMWBS and all four ONS4 measures (happiness, anxiety, life satisfaction, worthwhileness) from unconditional Bayesian growth curve models
Predicted pre/post well-being by age group (N=15,001 SWEMWBS; N=11,720 ONS4) from conditional growth models — older adults (70+) show slower improvement consistent with higher baselines
Predicted pre/post well-being by gender (N=15,001 SWEMWBS; N=11,720 ONS4) from conditional growth models — marginal sex differences in happiness and anxiety trajectories
